current_fact.fact_text: On May 1, 1947, gunmen opened fire on a May Day gathering at Portella della Ginestra in Sicily, killing 11 people and wounding dozens more. The attack targeted peasants and laborers celebrating workers’ rights and land reform, turning a spring festival into a political massacre. Its true masterminds remain disputed, tangled in a web of banditry, separatism, and Cold War power struggles.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

current_fact.match_reason: A politically murky massacre on a workers’ holiday invites re‑examination, echoing an episode devoted to unlearning and questioning the narratives we accept.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 1982, the British Royal Air Force launched Operation Black Buck, a series of long‑range bombing raids during the Falklands War. Vulcan bombers flew more than 12,000 kilometers round‑trip, refueled repeatedly in mid‑air, in one of the longest bombing missions in history. The operation was as much a feat of logistics and endurance as a display of military force.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The roaring engines and high‑stakes tension of a marathon bombing mission pair well with the episode’s surging, electric rock energy.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 2003, standing under a "Mission Accomplished" banner aboard the USS Abraham Lincoln, U.S. President George W. Bush declared that major combat operations in Iraq had ended. The words quickly became infamous as the conflict dragged on for years, far from resolved. The moment endures as a symbol of how narratives can race ahead of reality.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A premature declaration of success mirrors the episode’s exploration of polished, controlled surfaces and the tension between appearance and underlying reality.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 2018, the Syrian Democratic Forces resumed their Deir ez‑Zor campaign to clear the last pockets of the Islamic State from the Syria–Iraq border. It was the grinding endgame of a conflict fought village by village along a river that had seen civilizations rise and fall. Even as frontline maps shrank, the human and cultural scars remained enormous.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A complex borderland campaign in the Middle East connects with an episode that weaves Maghreb and global sounds, hinting at contested regions and shared cultural roots.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 1991, the MPLA government and UNITA rebels in Angola agreed to the Bicesse Accords, a peace deal that aimed to end a civil war that had raged since independence. The agreement promised multiparty elections and the integration of rival forces, offering a fragile hope after years of Cold War‑fueled conflict. Though fighting later resumed, it marked a crucial step toward the war’s eventual end in 2002.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A peace accord trying to untangle decades of proxy conflict echoes an episode that untangles the roots of a misunderstood genre shaped by colonial and post‑colonial histories.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 1994, three‑time Formula One world champion Ayrton Senna was killed when his car left the track at high speed during the San Marino Grand Prix. The crash shocked the racing world and led to major safety reforms in Formula One, from track design to car construction. Senna’s death turned him into a legend and changed how the sport thinks about risk, speed, and mortality.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

queue.match_reason: Senna’s arc from raw talent to mythic figure parallels an episode about evolution, fame, and the bittersweet shift from speed and noise to reflective distance.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 1999, the same day Mallory’s body was found on Everest, the mountain silently continued to record its own geological story — but it was in 305 CE that another kind of mountain of power shifted: Roman emperors Diocletian and Maximian both voluntarily retired from office. In a rare act in imperial history, they stepped down to try to stabilize the empire through a planned succession. Their experiment with power‑sharing and retirement ultimately failed, plunging Rome back into civil wars before Constantine rose to unify it again.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: The fading of imperial fire and the attempt to cool a burning empire match an episode about contrasting energies, balance, and the tensions within a single country’s identity.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 2003, while Bush spoke of "mission accomplished" at sea, another mission decades earlier had quietly begun reshaping our understanding of the universe: by the early 20th century, Vesto Slipher’s observations at Lowell Observatory — the same institution that would later name Pluto — helped reveal that many nebulae were actually distant galaxies racing away from us. His measurements of galactic redshifts laid crucial groundwork for the idea of an expanding universe, even if his name is less famous than Hubble’s.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: Slipher’s quiet, foundational work on an expanding universe suits an open‑ended Evening Flow introduction, both inviting listeners to drift beyond familiar boundaries.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 1945, as Berlin fell, the Soviet flag was hoisted over the Reich Chancellery — a photograph that would become an icon of victory. The image was later staged and retouched, with extra smoke added and a watch on a soldier’s wrist painted out to hide looting. Even history’s most famous moments, it turns out, can be part documentary, part careful editing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A heavily retouched victory photo contrasts with an episode that embraces imperfection, inviting us to notice what gets edited out of official history and art.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 2003, as speeches echoed over the Pacific, Iraqis were still living through the chaos of invasion, illustrating how different a war can look from above and below. That same tension — between the neatness of announcements and the mess of real life — runs through many modern conflicts. It asks uncomfortable questions about who gets to declare an ending, and for whom the fighting is truly over.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The gap between a declared end to war and the reality on the ground resonates with an episode about embracing slow, imperfect processes rather than neat, premature conclusions.

queue.fact_text: On May 1, 2018, while the Deir ez‑Zor campaign continued on Earth, radio waves carrying music, news, and voices were streaming past that war‑scarred region out into space, as they always do. Every broadcast is a tiny, unintentional message‑in‑a‑bottle to the cosmos, containing our conflicts, celebrations, and songs. Somewhere far away, an alien antenna could, in theory, pick up a fragment of that day and wonder what kind of planet sends such mixed signals.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A reflection on radio waves leaking into the cosmos matches Sedna FM’s first broadcast — a life soundtrack literally framed as launching our tiny signal into the universe.

published.fact_text: In 1930, the name "Pluto" for the newly discovered distant world was officially proposed by astronomer Vesto Slipher at Lowell Observatory. The suggestion had actually come from an 11‑year‑old English girl, Venetia Burney, who thought the Roman god of the underworld fit a dark, far‑off planet. The name spread so quickly that within months, Pluto had entered global imagination as the Solar System's ninth planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A newly named distant world and an episode about reimagining space and possibility both invite a mental ‘space shift’ toward the unknown.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1999, climbers on Mount Everest discovered the remarkably preserved body of British mountaineer George Mallory, missing since his 1924 attempt to reach the summit. The find revived one of mountaineering’s greatest mysteries: whether Mallory and his partner Andrew Irvine might have reached the top decades before the first confirmed ascent. To this day, that question remains unanswered, suspended in the thin air above the world’s highest peak.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: Mallory’s body lying frozen in rarefied air echoes the episode’s feeling of suspended immersion and quiet, mysterious depths.

published.fact_text: In 1978, Naomi Uemura became the first person to reach the North Pole alone, traveling by dog sled across the Arctic ice. He navigated shifting floes, brutal cold, and the psychological weight of complete isolation, guided only by his dogs, instruments, and determination. His journey redefined what a solo expedition could mean, pushing human endurance to the edge of the map.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode unfold in the Arctic circle, celebrating solitary courage and icy, cinematic landscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1753, Carl Linnaeus published "Species Plantarum", the book that formally launched modern plant taxonomy. In it, he gathered and organized thousands of plant species into a systematic naming scheme, giving each a two‑part Latin name. This simple but powerful idea became a universal language for botanists, letting humans around the world talk about the same plants with precision.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Linnaeus’ quiet reordering of the plant world pairs with the episode’s slow, contemplative atmosphere and its gentle, earth‑rooted mood.

published.fact_text: In 1898, during the Battle of Manila Bay, the U.S. Asiatic Squadron destroyed Spain’s Pacific fleet in just seven hours, with no American combat deaths. The clash was decisive not only militarily but symbolically, signaling the rise of the United States as a global naval power. It turned the waters around Manila into a turning point in both Spanish and American histories.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A historic naval showdown meets an episode built on life at sea; both revolve around ships, waves, and how the ocean shapes human stories.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1915, the luxury liner RMS Lusitania departed New York on what would be her final Atlantic crossing. Six days later, she was torpedoed off Ireland, sinking in just 18 minutes and killing nearly 1,200 people. The tragedy shocked public opinion and helped shift the world’s emotional tide toward the First World War.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: The Lusitania’s voyage from hope to catastrophe resonates with an episode about abrupt life changes, loss, and the long arc of recovery and reflection.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1929, the Kopet Dag earthquake struck the border region between Iran and Turkmenistan with a magnitude of 7.2 and violent shaking up to Mercalli intensity IX. Whole villages collapsed along the mountain front, and up to 3,800 people were killed in seconds. It remains a stark reminder that the Earth’s crust is not solid and still, but restless beneath our feet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, violent jolt from beneath the Earth parallels the episode’s theme of raw, electrifying shocks — energy you can feel in your bones.

published.fact_text: In 1945, German radio announced Adolf Hitler’s death on May 1, claiming he had fallen heroically at his command post as the Soviets raised their flag over the Reich Chancellery. The broadcast tried to spin a collapsing regime into a final mythic stand, even as Berlin lay in ruins. Within days, the Third Reich had formally surrendered, and the war in Europe was effectively over.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The fall of a fascist regime and its propaganda machine pairs with a fiercely political episode that channels anger into resistance and truth‑telling.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1960, a high‑altitude U.S. U‑2 spy plane piloted by Francis Gary Powers was shot down over Soviet territory during a reconnaissance mission. The incident shattered the illusion that such flights were untouchable and triggered a major Cold War diplomatic crisis. For a moment, one aircraft brought two superpowers dangerously close to open confrontation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Both the U‑2 incident and the episode revolve around technology, surveillance, and how invisible systems shape our sense of safety and connection.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1865, the Empire of Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay signed the Treaty of the Triple Alliance, joining forces against Paraguay in what would become one of South America's bloodiest wars. The conflict decimated Paraguay’s population and reshaped borders and power dynamics across the region. It stands as a stark example of how alliances can both protect and devastate smaller nations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A South American alliance and a show on Afrobeat both explore how regions forge identity and power through collective movements and cultural expression.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1486, Christopher Columbus laid out his bold proposal to Queen Isabella of Castile: sail west across the "Ocean Sea" to reach the riches of the Indies. He underestimated Earth’s size but overestimated his chances, and yet his pitch eventually won support and changed global history. It was a risky idea born from maps, imagination, and a willingness to step off the edge of the known world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: Columbus’ audacious westward plan mirrors the episode’s theme of travel as a mind‑shifting journey beyond familiar maps and rhythms.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1946, Indigenous pastoral workers in Western Australia's Pilbara region walked off sheep and cattle stations in a strike that would last nearly three years. They weren’t just demanding better pay; they were challenging a system that treated them as property rather than workers. Their quiet, stubborn refusal became one of Australia’s earliest and longest Indigenous rights campaigns.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: An Indigenous labour uprising in Australia aligns with Tuareg desert blues — both are stories of land, exploitation, and music as resistance in harsh landscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 2004, ten countries — including Cyprus, Czech Republic, Estonia, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Malta, Poland, Slovakia, and Slovenia — joined the European Union in its largest single expansion. Midnight celebrations rippled across the continent as borders softened and a new, more integrated Europe took shape. For many, it felt like stepping through a political doorway into a different future.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: The EU’s eastward expansion and a Mediterranean musical voyage both celebrate crossing borders and weaving diverse European shores into one shared journey.

published.fact_text: On May 1, 1947, gunmen opened fire on a May Day gathering at Portella della Ginestra in Sicily, killing 11 people and wounding dozens more. The attack targeted peasants and laborers celebrating workers’ rights and land reform, turning a spring festival into a political massacre. Its true masterminds remain disputed, tangled in a web of banditry, separatism, and Cold War power struggles.

episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?
